paper_title: Quantum AI for Cancer Diagnostic Biomarker Discovery

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly defines Sample$_3$ as the combination of Sample$_1$ and Sample$_2$. Changing the subscript to $_2$ creates a contradiction where the text claims Sample$_2$ is the 'combined gene set' despite having just defined it as the 'Hypomethylated + Upregulated' set only.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'Sample$_3$, representing the combined gene set, achieved the highest overall predictive performance', so changing $_3$ to $_2$ contradicts the stated sample label.

perturbations.why_wrong: The sentence establishes a mapping between Subtype-I/Subtype-II and the matrices Omic1.1/Omic1.2. Changing the first subscript to Subtype-II results in both matrices (1.1 and 1.2) being assigned to the same subtype, leaving Subtype-I without a corresponding matrix notation.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context shows 'Omic1.1${Subtype-I}$ and Omic1.2${Subtype-II}$', so the perturbed span changes Omic1.1 to be labeled Subtype-II instead of Subtype-I, contradicting the established pairing.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes the threshold as 'stringent' and intended to identify 'statistically robust features' using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. In statistical hypothesis testing, significance is achieved when the p-value is less than or equal to the alpha level; flipping the sign to $\ge 0.05$ would select non-significant genes, contradicting the goal of identifying robust biomarkers.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages consistently use < α (e.g., AdjP < α) as the significance threshold; changing ≤ 0.05 to ≥ 0.05 reverses the filtering direction, retaining non-significant genes instead of significant ones.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly defines the comparison as 'Tumor vs Normal'. The subscript TN is a standard abbreviation for Tumor-Normal used in the preceding step (1.1.1). Changing it to TT (Tumor-Tumor) creates an internal inconsistency with the verbal description of the comparison being performed.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly uses LUAD$_{TN}$ (Tumor vs Normal) twice in the same passage; changing TN to TT contradicts the established notation for the comparison.

perturbations.why_wrong: The definition of a 'LUAD-specific' gene set should require the gene to NOT be significant in LUSC. Changing the condition for LUSC_TN from >= alpha (not significant) to < alpha (significant) contradicts the definition of specificity described in the text.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original requires AdjP_LUSC_TN ≥ α (gene NOT significant in LUSC) to define LUAD-specificity; changing to < α makes the condition require LUSC significance, contradicting the LUAD-specific definition.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text defines LUAD-specific hypermethylated genes as those upregulated in LUAD (U1) but not in LUSC (U2). Changing the set difference operator to a union includes genes upregulated in LUSC, which violates the 'LUAD-specific' requirement.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original formula uses set difference (U_1 \ U_2) to exclude genes significant in LUSC; replacing with union (U_1 ∪ U_2) includes LUSC-significant genes, contradicting the LUAD-specific hypermethylation objective.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text defines $S_{g,k}$ as representing 'Statistical Significance'. In standard statistical practice described in the paper (false discovery rate control), significance is achieved when the adjusted p-value is below a threshold (typically 0.05). Flipping the operator to '>' means the model would treat statistically insignificant results as significant.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context establishes that significance means p_adj < 0.05; changing to > 0.05 reverses the condition, flagging non-significant genes as significant, contradicting standard statistical practice and the downstream use of S_{g,k}.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text defines significance in LUAD based on datasets F1 and F3. Using the min operator would require a gene to be significant in both datasets to be considered LUAD-significant, whereas the max operator (logical OR) correctly identifies a gene as significant if it appears in either relevant LUAD cohort.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original uses max(S_{g,F1}, S_{g,F3}), meaning a gene is LUAD-significant if significant in either F1 or F3; changing to min requires significance in both, contradicting the OR-logic intended.

perturbations.original:
\[
\begin{cases}
\text{LUAD-specific}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 0, \\
\text{Opposite both significant}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 1, \\
\text{Opposite weak}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 0 \text{ and/or } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 0.
\end{cases}
\]
perturbations.perturbed:
\[
\begin{cases}
\text{LUAD-specific}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 0, \\
\text{Opposite both significant}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 1, \\
\text{Opposite weak}, & \text{if } S_{\mathrm{LUAD}} = 1 \text{ and/or } S_{\mathrm{LUSC}} = 1.
\end{cases}
\]
perturbations.why_wrong: The definition of 'Opposite weak' is logically inconsistent with the other cases. If S_LUAD=1 and S_LUSC=1, it would fall under both 'Opposite both significant' and 'Opposite weak'. The context implies 'weak' should correspond to non-significance (S=0).
perturbations.reason: The original third case requires S_LUAD=0 and/or S_LUSC=0 (at least one is non-significant), but the perturbed version requires S_LUAD=1 and/or S_LUSC=1, making it overlap with the first two cases and contradicting the intended exhaustive classification.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text states that epsilon is used to avoid division by zero. Changing the sign to subtraction allows the denominator to become zero if the log term equals epsilon, defeating the stated purpose of the parameter.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context states ε is added to avoid division by zero; subtracting ε instead could make the denominator zero or negative when -log10(p_adj,F2,g) equals ε, contradicting the stated purpose of ε.

perturbations.why_wrong: The context describes imputing missing values for a feature using the median of that feature across samples. In the notation $X_{ij}$, $j$ typically represents the feature index and $i$ the sample index. Changing the subscript from $j$ to $i$ implies calculating the median across different features for a single sample (row-wise) rather than the median of the specific gene/feature across all samples (column-wise), which is inconsistent with standard imputation logic for genomic dataframes.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original uses median(X_j) where j indexes features/columns; changing to X_i (row/sample index) means imputing with the median across features for a sample rather than the median across samples for a feature, contradicting standard column-wise imputation.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that the data were split into 80% training and 20% testing. Setting test_size to 0.8 results in 20% training and 80% testing, contradicting the prose.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'Data were split into 80% training and 20% testing', so test_size=0.8 contradicts the stated 20% test split.

perturbations.why_wrong: The paper defines X_DNA as having 128 features and X_RNA as having 128 features. The concatenation of these two feature sets must result in 256 features (128 + 128), not 128.
perturbations.reason: The surrounding_context shows both X_DNA and X_RNA have 128 features each, and the test set concatenation yields ×256; the perturbed train set dimension of 128 contradicts the concatenation of two 128-feature matrices which must yield 256.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly defines the F1-score as the 'harmonic mean' of precision and recall. The harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$ is $2ab/(a+b)$. Changing the constant from 2 to 1 makes the formula mathematically inconsistent with the definition of a harmonic mean provided in the preceding sentence.
perturbations.reason: The standard F1-score formula requires the factor 2 to make it the harmonic mean of precision and recall; changing 2 to 1 produces a different (incorrect) formula contradicting the stated definition of F1 as the harmonic mean.
